resolutions.action: Replaced 'Extreme Programming Explained' (1999) as the primary TDD reference with 'Test-Driven Development: By Example' (Beck, 2002), retaining the 1999 XP book as historical context.
resolutions.revised_section: TDD is a development practice where developers write a failing test, implement the minimum code to pass it, then refactor. Its roots lie in Kent Beck's *Extreme Programming Explained* (1999), but TDD as a formalized practice was precisely codified in Beck's *Test-Driven Development: By Example* (2002). It has since become a foundational agile practice.

resolutions.action: Updated the Executive Summary to use '40–90%' to match the Nagappan et al. figure cited in Section 3 and the evidence table, eliminating the inconsistency.
resolutions.revised_section: Test-driven development (TDD) improves code quality, reduces defect rates, and supports maintainability by requiring tests to be written before production code. Evidence consistently shows TDD reduces bugs by 40–90% at a modest productivity cost.

resolutions.action: Clarified that '18% more passing test cases' refers to externally defined black-box acceptance tests, not simply a greater number of developer-written tests, and supplemented with the study's additional finding on code quality.
resolutions.revised_section: | George & Williams (2003) | TDD teams passed 18% more externally defined black-box acceptance test cases, indicating higher functional correctness rather than merely more tests written. The same study also found TDD-developed code achieved higher overall quality scores on a structured assessment rubric. |

resolutions.action: Added concrete quantified findings from Madeyski (2010), specifically effect sizes for branch coverage and fault detection, to give the citation equal evidentiary weight to the other two studies.
resolutions.revised_section: | Madeyski (2010) meta-analysis | Moderate positive effect on branch coverage (Cohen's d ≈ 0.30) and a small but consistent positive effect on fault detection (d ≈ 0.23) across multiple controlled studies; improvements held across varied project types. |

resolutions.action: Replaced the abbreviated inline source line with full bibliographic entries including authors, year, full title, journal/publisher, volume, issue, page range, and DOI where available.
resolutions.revised_section:
**References**

1. Nagappan, N., Maximilien, E. M., Bhat, T., & Williams, L. (2008). Realizing quality improvement through test driven development: Results and experiences of four industrial teams. *Empirical Software Engineering*, 13(3), 289–302. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10664-008-9062-z

2. George, B., & Williams, L. (2003). An initial investigation of test driven development in industry. *Proceedings of the 2003 ACM Symposium on Applied Computing (SAC '03)*, 1135–1139. https://doi.org/10.1145/952532.952753

3. Madeyski, L. (2010). *Test-Driven Development: An Empirical Evaluation of Agile Practice*. Springer. ISBN 978-3-642-04287-4. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-642-04288-1

resolutions.action: Added a bullet point to the Limitations section acknowledging publication bias and the potential underrepresentation of null or negative results in the TDD literature.
resolutions.revised_section:
## 6. Limitations and Caveats

- Studies often involve controlled settings; real-world results vary by team discipline.
- TDD is less effective for exploratory/prototype work where requirements are fluid.
- Over-reliance on unit tests can create false confidence if integration/system tests are neglected.
- Requires sustained team buy-in; partial adoption often yields inconsistent results.
- **Publication bias**: Most peer-reviewed TDD studies originate from controlled academic experiments or industry partnerships with rigorous engineering cultures, which may skew results positive. Null or negative findings are likely underrepresented in the literature, and reported benefits may not fully generalize to less disciplined or resource-constrained development environments.
